title: Reading a Payoff Diagram
html:
Every option strategy in this part is a recipe of legs — calls and puts, bought or sold, at chosen strikes. The single most important skill is reading the payoff diagram: a picture of your profit or loss at expiration as a function of the underlying price. Master this one chart type and every strategy that follows becomes a variation on a theme.

This is education, not financial advice. The numbers are illustrative and ignore commissions, assignment quirks and taxes unless stated.

The anatomy of the chart

The horizontal axis is the stock price S at expiration. The vertical axis is profit and loss (P/L) in dollars per share — multiply by 100 for one standard equity contract. The thick line is your payoff. Three landmarks matter on every diagram:

  • Break-even (BE): where the payoff line crosses zero. Above or below it you make money.
  • Max profit: the highest the line ever reaches (sometimes infinite).
  • Max loss: the lowest the line ever reaches (sometimes infinite).
Definition.
A long call's value at expiration is its intrinsic value: payoff = max(S − K, 0). Subtract the premium paid to get P/L: P/L = max(S − K, 0) − premium.

The long call, annotated

Consider buying one ACME 100-strike call for a premium of $3. You pay $300. Below the strike the call expires worthless and you lose the whole premium — a flat floor at −$3. Above the strike each dollar of stock adds a dollar of intrinsic value. You only turn a profit once the stock has risen enough to recover the premium.

Read the chart left to right. From far left up to the strike at 100 the line sits flat at −$3 — this is the max loss, the entire premium, realized anywhere below 100. The line begins climbing at the strike, the kink in the hockey stick. It crosses zero — the break-even — at 103, the strike plus the premium. From there it rises one-for-one with the stock, so the max profit is unlimited: the line never turns back down.

Long call P/L worked three ways
Premium paid = $3   Strike K = 100 S = 95: max(95−100,0) − 3 = 0 − 3 = −$3 (max loss) S = 103: max(103−100,0) − 3 = 3 − 3 = $0 (break-even) S = 115: max(115−100,0) − 3 = 15 − 3 = +$12 profit

Four reflexes for any new strategy

  1. List the legs. Long or short, call or put, which strike, how many.
  2. Net the premium. A net debit means you paid (cash out); a net credit means you collected (cash in). Debit strategies generally have a max loss equal to the debit; credit strategies often cap profit at the credit.
  3. Find the kinks. Payoff lines bend only at strikes. Between strikes the line is straight, so you only need the value at each strike to draw the whole picture.
  4. Walk the extremes. Ask what happens at S = 0 and at S → ∞. That tells you whether max profit or max loss is bounded or infinite.
Insight.
Long options (debit) define your risk to the premium but bleed value over time. Short options (credit) collect premium and benefit from time decay, but the risk is often larger than the reward. The payoff shape tells you which side of that trade you are on at a glance.
Pitfall.
The payoff diagram shows P/L at expiration only. Before expiry the position is worth more or less because of remaining time value and implied volatility. A long call can be deeply underwater at expiration's strike yet show a gain a month earlier if volatility spiked. Treat the diagram as the destination, not the journey.

Short positions flip the picture

Selling the same 100 call mirrors the buyer. Your best case is keeping the $3 credit (a flat ceiling), your break-even is still 103, but above it your losses are unlimited. Whenever you sell a leg, picture the buyer's chart reflected through the horizontal axis — your gain is their loss and vice versa.

▸Try It

Goal: read a payoff cold and label its three landmarks.

  1. Buy a put: a long 100 put for a $2 premium. On paper, compute P/L at S = 90, 98 and 105 using P/L = max(K − S, 0) − premium.
  2. Mark the break-even (98), the max loss (−$2 above 100) and the max profit (at S = 0, equal to 98).
  3. Now sell that put and redraw it as the mirror image. Confirm the seller's max profit is the $2 credit and the max loss is 98.

title: The Covered Call and the Cash-Secured Put
html:
The covered call and the cash-secured put are the two foundational income strategies. Each sells one option backed by an asset you already hold — shares for the call, cash for the put — so neither is naked. They are mirror images, and seeing why is the lesson.

The covered call

You own 100 shares of ACME bought at $50. You are mildly bullish but content to sell higher, so you sell one 55-strike call expiring in a month and collect $1.50 ($150). You are covered because if the stock is called away you simply deliver the shares you already own.

Definition.
Covered call = long 100 shares + short 1 call. Net cost basis falls to entry − premium = 50 − 1.50 = $48.50. Max profit = (strike − basis) = 55 − 48.50 = $6.50, capped. Upside above the strike is forfeited.

Read the chart. Below 48.50 you lose money, but less than holding stock alone — the $1.50 premium shifts your break-even down from 50 to 48.50, the new floor of profitability. Between 48.50 and 55 you gain on the shares plus keep the premium. At and above 55 the line goes flat: the short call caps you at $6.50 max profit (a gain to 55 of $5 plus the $1.50 premium, i.e. an effective sale at 56.50). You gave up unlimited upside in exchange for income and a small downside buffer.

Covered call outcomes at expiration
Basis 50, premium 1.50, strike 55 S = 45: stock −5.00 + premium 1.50 = −$3.50 S = 48.50: stock −1.50 + premium 1.50 = $0 (break-even) S = 53: stock +3.00 + premium 1.50 = +$4.50 S = 60: capped at 55: gain 5.00 + premium 1.50 = +$6.50 (max)

The cash-secured put

Now the mirror. Instead of owning ACME, you would happily buy it at 45. You sell one 45-strike put and collect, say, $1.50, setting aside $4,500 of cash to honor the purchase if assigned. That cash is the security; the put is not naked.

If ACME stays above 45 the put expires worthless and you keep the $150 — your max profit. If it falls below 45 you are assigned and buy 100 shares, but the premium lowers your effective entry to $43.50 (the break-even). Your max loss is large but bounded: if the stock went to zero you would lose 43.50 per share, exactly what an outright buyer at 43.50 would lose. The risk is identical to owning the stock from your target price.

Insight.
A cash-secured put has the same payoff shape as a covered call. Both are short a put in disguise (the covered call is synthetically short a 55 put). They collect premium, profit in a quiet-to-rising market, and carry full downside below the strike. Choose the put when you want to acquire stock cheaply; choose the call when you already own it and want income.

Pitfall.
The covered call's worst feature is opportunity cost. If ACME gaps to 70 on news, you are still capped at 56.50 and watch the rest evaporate. Sell calls only at strikes where you are genuinely willing to part with the shares, and avoid selling through earnings unless that capped outcome is acceptable.
▸Try It

Goal: prove the two strategies are mirror twins.

  1. For the covered call (basis 50, short 55 call at 1.50), tabulate P/L at S = 40, 50, 55, 65.
  2. For the cash-secured 45 put at 1.50, tabulate P/L at the same prices.
  3. Note where each caps out and where each break-even sits, and confirm both are short-volatility, premium-collecting positions.

title: The Protective Put and the Collar
html:
Where the last lesson sold options for income, this one buys them for insurance. The protective put hedges a stock you own; the collar pays for that hedge by selling away some upside. Together they let a long-term holder sleep through a storm.

The protective put

You own 100 shares of ACME at $50 and fear a drawdown but do not want to sell. You buy one 45-strike put for $1.50. That put is a contractual right to sell at 45 no matter how far the stock falls — downside insurance with a deductible.

Definition.
Protective put = long stock + long put. Max loss = (entry − strike) + premium = (50 − 45) + 1.50 = $6.50, fixed. Break-even = entry + premium = $51.50. Upside remains unlimited, minus the premium drag.

Read the floor. Below 45 the put gains a dollar for every dollar the stock loses, so the combined line goes flat: your max loss is locked at $6.50 however ugly the tape gets. Between 45 and 51.50 you are recovering the insurance premium; above 51.50 you profit, participating fully in any rally less the $1.50 you spent. The cost of the insurance is that drag — you start each move 1.50 behind.

Protective put vs. naked stock
Stock basis 50, put strike 45, premium 1.50 S = 35: stock −15.00, put +10.00, prem −1.50 = −$6.50 (floored) S = 45: stock −5.00, put 0, prem −1.50 = −$6.50 S = 51.50: stock +1.50, put 0, prem −1.50 = $0 (break-even) S = 60: stock +10.00, put 0, prem −1.50 = +$8.50

Financing it: the collar

Insurance is a recurring cost. To offset it, sell an out-of-the-money call and use that credit to pay for the put. Sell the 55 call for $1.50 against your long stock and long 45 put. The call premium exactly funds the put premium — a zero-cost collar.

Definition.
Collar = long stock + long put (floor) + short call (ceiling). Here floor 45, ceiling 55, net premium ≈ $0. P/L is boxed between −$5 (at or below 45) and +$5 (at or above 55).

The collar trades both tails for certainty. Below 45 the long put floors your loss at $5; above 55 the short call caps your gain at $5. In between you ride the stock normally. Because the call paid for the put, you spent nothing upfront — you simply rented out the upside above 55 to buy protection below 45.

Insight.
A collar is a covered call with a long put bolted on, or equivalently a bull call spread expressed on stock. It is the workhorse of holders sitting on large unrealized gains who want to protect a position into an uncertain event without triggering a taxable sale.
Pitfall.
A zero-cost collar is not free — you pay in surrendered upside. If ACME is acquired at 70, the short call caps you at 55 and you miss $15. Widen the call strike (accept a small net debit) when you want more room to run, or narrow the put strike (a higher floor) when protection matters most.
▸Try It

Goal: tune a collar to your risk tolerance.

  1. Start from long stock at 50. Add a 45 put at 1.50 and confirm the floored max loss of 6.50.
  2. Sell a 55 call at 1.50 to zero out the cost; verify the box from −5 to +5.
  3. Now move the call to 60 (collect only ~0.60). Recompute the small net debit and the wider ceiling, and note how the upside room grows.

title: Vertical Spreads: Bull and Bear, Call and Put
html:
A vertical spread buys one option and sells another of the same type and expiration at a different strike. The short leg pays for part of the long leg, cutting cost and defining risk — at the price of capping profit. There are exactly four, and once you can build one you can build all of them.

The four verticals

SpreadLegsCash flowView
Bull calllong lower call, short higher callnet debitbullish
Bear putlong higher put, short lower putnet debitbearish
Bull putshort higher put, long lower putnet creditbullish / neutral
Bear callshort lower call, long higher callnet creditbearish / neutral

The bull call spread

You are bullish on ACME near 50. Buy the 50 call for $3 and sell the 55 call for $1, a net debit of $2. The sold call subsidizes the purchase, but in return you give up everything above 55.

Definition.
Bull call spread: max loss = net debit = $2; max profit = (width − debit) = (5 − 2) = $3; break-even = lower strike + debit = $52.

The chart is a staircase. Below 50 both calls expire worthless and you lose the $2 debit — flat max loss. From 50 to 55 the long call gains while the short is still out of the money, so the line climbs. It crosses zero at the 52 break-even and flattens at 55, where the short call's losses start exactly offsetting further long-call gains, locking the $3 max profit. Your risk-reward is 2 risked to make 3.

Bull call spread at expiration
+50 call ($3), −55 call ($1), net debit $2 S = 48: both worthless = −$2 (max loss) S = 52: long +2, short 0, debit −2 = $0 (break-even) S = 55: long +5, short 0, debit −2 = +$3 (max) S = 60: long +10, short −5, debit −2 = +$3 (capped)

The bear put spread

The bearish mirror. Buy the 50 put for $3 and sell the 45 put for $1, again a $2 debit. You profit as ACME falls toward 45, with the same defined geometry reflected.

Here max loss is the $2 debit (stock above 50), max profit $3 (stock at or below 45), and break-even is 48 — the long strike minus the debit. Same risk-reward, opposite direction.

Credit verticals: selling the spread

Reverse the cash flow and you get the credit spreads. A bull put spread sells the 50 put and buys the 45 put for a net credit; you keep it if ACME stays above 50, and your max loss is the width minus the credit. A bear call spread sells the 50 call and buys the 55 call for a credit, profiting if ACME stays below 50. Credit verticals win from time decay and falling volatility, which is why they shine in high-IV regimes — a theme we return to in 12.10.

Insight.
A debit spread and its mirror-strike credit spread are the same position. Long the 50/55 call spread for a $2 debit is identical in payoff to selling the 50/55 put spread for a $3 credit. Trade whichever has better fills and pin risk; the economics are the same.
Pitfall.
Width matters. A 5-point spread for a $2 debit risks $2 to make $3 (1.5:1). A 5-point spread for a $4 debit risks $4 to make $1 — a poor 0.25:1 payout that needs to be right most of the time. Always express the spread as risk-to-reward, not just dollars.
▸Try It

Goal: build all four verticals from one strike ladder.

  1. Using strikes 45/50/55 on ACME at 50, write the legs for a bull call, bear put, bull put and bear call spread.
  2. For each, compute net debit/credit, max profit, max loss and break-even.
  3. Pair the bull call (debit) with the matching bull put (credit) and confirm their payoffs are identical.

title: Straddles and Strangles
html:
Vertical spreads bet on direction. Straddles and strangles bet on magnitude: they profit from a big move either way and lose if the stock sits still. These are pure volatility plays, and the central question is always whether the move you expect is bigger than the move the premium has already priced in.

The long straddle

ACME sits at 50 before an earnings report. You expect a violent move but have no idea which way. Buy the 50 call for $3 and the 50 put for $3 — same strike, same expiry — for a total debit of $6. You now own a position that wins on a large move in either direction.

Definition.
Long straddle = long ATM call + long ATM put. Cost = combined premium = $6 = max loss (at S = strike). Break-evens = strike ± cost = 44 and 56. Profit is unlimited above 56 and large (to 44) below.

Read the V. The single worst outcome is the stock pinning the strike at exactly 50, where both options expire worthless and you lose the full $6 — the bottom of the valley. Move away from 50 in either direction and one leg gains intrinsic value. The position breaks even at 44 and 56 (the strike plus or minus the $6 cost) and profits beyond them: unlimited to the upside, and down to a price of 44 on the downside. You do not need to guess direction — only that the move exceeds $6.

Long straddle outcomes
+50 call ($3) + 50 put ($3), total cost $6 S = 50: both worthless = −$6 (max loss) S = 56: call +6, put 0, cost −6 = $0 (upper BE) S = 44: call 0, put +6, cost −6 = $0 (lower BE) S = 62: call +12, put 0, cost −6 = +$6

The long strangle

A straddle is expensive because both options are at the money. Widen the strikes to cheapen it: buy the 55 call for $1.50 and the 45 put for $1.50, a debit of just $3. This is a strangle — half the cost, but it needs a bigger move to pay off.

The strangle's payoff has a flat trough between 45 and 55 — anywhere in that range both options expire worthless and you lose the full $3 debit. The break-evens are 42 and 58: the put strike minus the cost and the call strike plus the cost. You paid half the straddle's premium, but the move needed to break even is larger (you must clear 58 versus 56). The trade-off is structural: lower cost, lower probability of a big enough move.

Insight.
The make-or-break is implied vs. realized volatility. The $6 straddle is the market's priced-in expected move. You profit only if the stock moves more than the premium already discounts. Buying a straddle into already-elevated earnings IV often loses even on a real move, because IV collapses afterward (the volatility crush) and drains both legs.
Pitfall.
These are long-theta-negative positions: every day of calm bleeds time value from both legs. A straddle held through a quiet week loses money even if your eventual thesis is right. Sellers take the opposite side — a short straddle or strangle collects the premium and profits from stillness, but with unlimited risk, so it is only for the well-margined and risk-aware.
▸Try It

Goal: compare a straddle and strangle on the same expected move.

  1. Price the 50 straddle ($6) and the 45/55 strangle ($3); write each pair of break-evens.
  2. Suppose ACME moves to 59 after earnings. Compute the P/L of each and see which captured more of the move.
  3. Now suppose it moves only to 52. Compute both P/Ls and confirm both lose — the move was too small for either.

title: The Iron Condor and Iron Butterfly
html:
If the straddle buyer bets on a big move, the iron condor and iron butterfly are the sellers' answer: defined-risk credit strategies that profit when the stock stays put. They are the bread and butter of range-bound, premium-selling traders.

The iron condor

ACME is rangebound near 50. You sell an out-of-the-money strangle — short the 55 call and short the 45 put — to collect premium, then buy a 60 call and a 40 put as wings to cap the risk. The wings turn unlimited short-strangle risk into a defined-risk box.

Definition.
Iron condor = bull put spread (short 45 / long 40 put) + bear call spread (short 55 / long 60 call). Net credit here = $2. Max profit = credit = $2. Max loss = wing width − credit = (5 − 2) = $3. Profit zone = short strikes ± credit = 43 to 57.

Read the plateau. Between the short strikes 45 and 55 all four legs are out of the money, so you keep the entire $2 credit — the flat max profit roof. The position stays profitable out to the 43 and 57 break-evens (the short strikes widened by the credit). Beyond the wings at 40 and 60 the loss is fully capped at $3. You are paid to bet ACME finishes inside a band, and the wings guarantee you cannot be hurt by a tail event.

Iron condor at expiration
Short 45 put / 55 call, long 40 put / 60 call, credit $2 S = 50: all worthless, keep credit = +$2 (max) S = 57: call spread −2, credit +2 = $0 (upper BE) S = 43: put spread −2, credit +2 = $0 (lower BE) S = 62: call spread −5, credit +2 = −$3 (max loss)

The iron butterfly

Pull the short strikes together to the money and you get an iron butterfly: short the 50 call and short the 50 put (an at-the-money short straddle), with long 45 put and long 55 call wings. Because the short options are ATM, you collect far more credit — say $4 — but the profitable range is narrower.

The iron butterfly's payoff is a tent peaking at the 50 strike, where both short options expire worthless and you keep the full $4. Break-evens are 46 and 54 (strike ± credit), and max loss is the wing width minus credit, (5 − 4) = $1. Compared with the condor it offers a higher max profit and smaller max loss, but a much narrower target — it wants the stock to pin 50, whereas the condor is happy anywhere in a wide band.

Insight.
Both structures are short volatility and short theta-positive: they decay in your favor and gain when implied volatility falls. Sell them when IV is rich and you expect a quiet drift. The condor trades a wider, safer range for a smaller credit; the butterfly trades a tighter target for a fatter credit and a tiny max loss.
Pitfall.
Range strategies have an asymmetric feel: many small wins, occasional larger losses. A single trend day can blow through a short strike. Manage by closing or rolling when price tests a short strike, and size so a max-loss outcome is survivable — the defined risk is real but it is still 1.5× your credit on the condor.
▸Try It

Goal: contrast the condor and butterfly.

  1. Write out the four legs of the 40/45/55/60 condor and the 45/50/55 iron butterfly.
  2. Compute credit, max profit, max loss and break-evens for each.
  3. Decide which you would prefer if you expected ACME to hover tightly around 50 versus drift somewhere within 45-55, and justify it.

title: Long and Short Butterflies
html:
A butterfly is a precision instrument. Built from three strikes in a 1–2–1 ratio, it targets a single price at expiration for a tiny cost and a high potential payoff. The long butterfly is a cheap bet on stillness; the short butterfly is a bet on movement.

The long call butterfly

You think ACME, at 50, will be near 50 at expiration. Build a 45/50/55 long call butterfly: buy one 45 call, sell two 50 calls, buy one 55 call. The two short calls finance the wings, so the whole structure costs only a small net debit — say $1.

Definition.
Long butterfly = +1 lower / −2 middle / +1 upper, equal spacing. Max loss = net debit = $1 (outside the wings). Max profit = wing width − debit = (5 − 1) = $4, achieved only at the body strike. Break-evens = body ± (max profit) = 46 and 54.

The payoff is a tent with its apex precisely at 50. There, the long 45 call is worth $5 while the two short 50 calls and long 55 call expire worthless, netting $5 of intrinsic minus the $1 debit for $4 max profit. Stray to the wings (45 or 55) and the structure decays back to the $1 cost. Outside the wings everything cancels and you simply lose the $1 debit — the flat max loss. You risk $1 to make $4 if your pin is right: a 4:1 payout that rewards precision.

Long butterfly at expiration
+45 call, −2×50 call, +55 call, debit $1 S = 42: all worthless = −$1 (max loss) S = 46: +45 worth 1, debit −1 = $0 (lower BE) S = 50: +45 worth 5, shorts 0, debit −1 = +$4 (max) S = 54: +45 worth 9, −2×4, debit −1 = $0 (upper BE)

The short butterfly

Invert every leg — sell one 45 call, buy two 50 calls, sell one 55 call — and you have a short butterfly, taken in for a credit. It is the mirror: it profits when the stock moves away from the body and loses if it pins 50.

Here the picture flips: a valley at 50 where you suffer the max loss of (width − credit) = $4, and flat plateaus of +$1 max profit outside the wings, where you keep the credit. The short butterfly is a low-cost, defined-risk way to bet against a pin — a cousin of the strangle, but cheaper and capped.

Insight.
A butterfly can be built from calls or puts with an identical payoff (put-call parity guarantees it). It can also be read as two adjacent verticals: the long call butterfly is a bull 45/50 call spread plus a bear 50/55 call spread sharing the 50 strike. Seeing it as two spreads makes the geometry obvious.
Pitfall.
The headline 4:1 payout is misleading because the bullseye is tiny — full profit needs the stock to land essentially on 50 at expiration. Realistically you close early for a fraction of max. Also beware pin risk: if ACME settles right at 50, your short legs may or may not be assigned, leaving an unhedged stock position over the weekend. Close butterflies before expiration to avoid that ambiguity.
▸Try It

Goal: see the butterfly as two verticals and price its edges.

  1. Split the 45/50/55 long call butterfly into a 45/50 bull call spread and a 50/55 bear call spread; confirm the leg counts match.
  2. Tabulate P/L at S = 44, 50, 56 and verify max loss −$1 and max profit +$4.
  3. Now flip to the short butterfly and confirm the payoff is the exact mirror.

title: Calendar and Diagonal Spreads
html:
Every strategy so far used a single expiration. Calendar and diagonal spreads add a second one, selling a near-term option against a longer-dated one. They harvest the fact that time decay accelerates as expiration nears — the front-month option bleeds theta faster than the back-month you own.

Why theta is uneven

An option's time value does not decay linearly. It erodes slowly when far out and then collapses in the final weeks, roughly with the square root of time remaining. Sell the fast-decaying front month and own the slow-decaying back month, and the difference accrues to you while both share the same strike.

Definition.
Calendar (horizontal) spread = short near-term option + long longer-term option, same strike. Established for a net debit. Profit comes from the front leg decaying faster (positive net theta) and from a rise in implied volatility (positive net vega).

The long call calendar

ACME is at 50 and you expect it to stay near there for a few weeks. Sell the front-month 50 call for $1.50 and buy the back-month 50 call for $3, a net debit of $1.50. Each day the front call decays faster than the back call, widening the spread's value in your favor — provided the stock cooperates by staying near 50.

Calendar mechanics at front-month expiry
Short front 50 call $1.50, long back 50 call $3, debit $1.50 S near 50: front expires ~worthless (you keep 1.50), back retains time value → net gain S = 38 (far below): both calls ~worthless → lose most of the $1.50 debit S = 65 (far above): both deep ITM, values converge → spread shrinks toward ~debit loss Best case: S pins the strike at front expiry

Because the two legs do not expire together, a calendar's true payoff cannot be drawn as a clean expiration hockey stick — its value at the front-month expiry depends on the back-month's remaining time value. But the shape resembles a tent centered on the strike, much like a long butterfly: max value when the stock sits at 50, decaying toward the debit as the stock runs far in either direction.

This decay curve is the calendar's whole thesis. The front-month option you are short lives at the right end of the curve, where the slope is steepest and value falls fastest. The back-month you are long sits further left, decaying gently. You pocket the difference in slopes — as long as the stock does not run away and rob the strike of its relevance.

The diagonal spread

A diagonal changes both the expiration and the strike — for example, sell the front-month 52 call and buy the back-month 50 call. It blends a calendar's time-decay edge with a vertical's directional lean. A call diagonal carries a mild bullish bias (you want a gentle grind up toward the short strike), making it a flexible way to express both a view and a volatility opinion.

Insight.
Calendars are long vega: they gain if implied volatility rises because the longer-dated leg has more vega than the front leg. Put them on when IV is low and you expect it to climb into an event, with the stock pinned near the strike. They are among the few strategies that profit from both calm price action and rising volatility at once.
Pitfall.
A calendar's risk is a big move in either direction, which flattens the tent and erases the debit. It is also short gamma near the strike at front expiry — a sudden jump hurts. And rolling the short leg forward each cycle racks up commissions and assignment risk. Treat calendars as positions to manage actively, not set-and-forget.
▸Try It

Goal: reason about a calendar's behavior through time.

  1. Set up the 50 calendar: short front 50 call $1.50, long back 50 call $3, debit $1.50.
  2. Describe what happens to each leg if ACME sits at 50 until front-month expiry, then if it gaps to 65.
  3. Convert it to a diagonal by moving the short leg to the 52 strike and explain the added directional tilt.

title: Ratio Spreads
html:
A ratio spread breaks the one-to-one symmetry of a vertical by selling more contracts than it buys. That extra short option cheapens the trade — often financing it to zero cost or a credit — but it also leaves a naked leg, introducing an uneven and sometimes unlimited risk zone. Ratio spreads are powerful and treacherous in equal measure.

The 1×2 call ratio

ACME is at 50 and you are moderately bullish — you think it drifts toward 55 but no higher. Buy one 50 call for $3 and sell two 55 calls for $1 each, collecting $2 against the $3 paid for a net debit of just $1. One of the two short calls is covered by the long call; the second is naked.

Definition.
1×2 call ratio = +1 lower call, −2 higher calls. Below the lower strike: lose the net debit. Peak profit at the upper strike = (strike width) + credit − debit = (5) − 1 = $4. Above the upper strike the extra short call creates an upside risk zone; the upper break-even = upper strike + max profit = 59, beyond which losses are unlimited.

Walk the chart. Below 50 everything expires worthless and you lose the small $1 debit. From 50 to 55 the long call gains while both shorts stay out of the money, climbing to the peak profit of $4 right at 55. Above 55 the two short calls together lose two dollars for every dollar the long gains one — a net loss of one dollar per point — so the line turns down. It re-crosses zero at the upper break-even of 59 and then keeps falling: above 59 the position has unlimited risk because you are effectively short one naked call.

1×2 call ratio at expiration
+50 call ($3), −2×55 call ($1 each), net debit $1 S = 48: all worthless = −$1 (debit) S = 55: long +5, shorts 0, debit −1 = +$4 (peak) S = 59: long +9, shorts −2×4, debit −1 = $0 (upper BE) S = 70: long +20, shorts −2×15, debit −1 = −$11 and falling

The put ratio and the front spread

The bearish mirror is the 1×2 put ratio: buy one 50 put, sell two 45 puts. It peaks at the lower strike and carries a downside risk zone if the stock collapses past the lower break-even. When a ratio is established for a net credit rather than a debit, traders call it a front spread: you cannot lose on the side away from the strikes (you keep the credit), and your only real risk lies in the runaway direction.

Insight.
Read a 1×2 ratio as a vertical spread plus an extra naked short option. The +50/−55 call vertical gives the rising left side; the additional short 55 call adds the income that cheapens it and the unlimited right-tail risk. This decomposition tells you exactly where the danger lives and how a sharp move would hurt.
Pitfall.
The naked leg is the whole story. A 1×2 call ratio looks benign and may even be a credit, but a takeover gap to 75 turns that tidy diagram into a six-figure loss. Many brokers require naked-option approval and heavy margin for exactly this reason. If you trade ratios, consider capping the tail with a far wing (turning it into a defined-risk ratioed butterfly), and never run them through binary events.
▸Try It

Goal: map the danger zone of a ratio.

  1. For the +50 / −2×55 call ratio at a $1 debit, compute P/L at S = 48, 55, 59 and 68.
  2. Identify the peak, the upper break-even and the slope of the right tail (loss per point above 55).
  3. Add a long 60 call to cap the tail; describe how the unlimited risk becomes defined and what it costs in peak profit.

title: Choosing a Strategy from Outlook and IV
html:
You now own a toolbox of a dozen strategies. The final skill is selection: matching the right structure to two inputs — your directional outlook (bullish, neutral, bearish) and the implied-volatility regime (high or low). Get these two axes right and the strategy almost picks itself.

The two questions

Before any trade, answer:

  1. Which way, and how far? Strongly directional, mildly directional, or going nowhere? This sets the skeleton — call structures for up, put structures for down, balanced structures for sideways.
  2. Is volatility rich or cheap? Compare current IV to its own history (IV rank/percentile). High IV favors selling premium (credit, short-vega); low IV favors buying it (debit, long-vega).
Rule of thumb.
High IV → be a net seller (collect inflated premium, profit as IV reverts down). Low IV → be a net buyer (cheap premium, profit as IV expands). Then overlay your direction to choose calls vs. puts vs. balanced.

The selection grid

OutlookHigh IV (sell premium)Low IV (buy premium)
BullishBull put spread (credit); cash-secured putBull call spread (debit); long call
NeutralIron condor; iron butterfly; short strangleLong calendar; long butterfly
BearishBear call spread (credit)Bear put spread (debit); long put
Big move, no direction(avoid buying) sell premium only if you expect calmLong straddle / strangle

Notice the pattern: in each cell the direction picks calls or puts, while the IV regime picks whether you sell a credit spread or buy a debit spread. A bullish view in high IV becomes a bull put spread; the same view in low IV becomes a bull call spread. Both lean bullish; they differ only in how they treat volatility.

Worked selection: bullish into high IV

ACME trades at 50, you are bullish, and IV is in the 80th percentile after a scare. Buying calls would overpay for inflated premium and suffer if IV mean-reverts. Instead sell a bull put spread: short the 48 put, long the 43 put, for a $1.50 credit. You profit if ACME holds above 48, you are short vega (gaining as IV falls), and your risk is defined at (width − credit) = (5 − 1.50) = $3.50.

The payoff is a credit spread's plateau: keep the full $1.50 anywhere above 48, break even at 46.50 (short strike minus credit), and bottom out at the −$3.50 max loss below 43. You expressed a bullish view, sold expensive volatility, and capped your risk — three boxes ticked at once. Had IV instead been cheap, the right answer would flip to a long call or a bull call debit spread, buying the cheap premium for unlimited or defined upside.

Matching the trade to the regime
Bullish + high IV: bull put spread (credit, short vega) — profit if stock holds, IV falls Bullish + low IV: bull call spread / long call (debit, long vega) — profit if stock rises, IV rises Neutral + high IV: iron condor — profit if stock stalls, IV falls Neutral + low IV: calendar / butterfly — profit if stock pins, IV rises Always: let direction pick call vs. put; let IV pick buy vs. sell
Insight.
The grid is a starting map, not a straitjacket. Refine with your conviction (defined-risk spreads when unsure, fuller exposure when confident), your time horizon (theta works for sellers, against buyers), and the event calendar (earnings inflate IV and then crush it). Strategy selection is the disciplined intersection of view, volatility, and risk tolerance.
Pitfall.
The classic error is fighting the regime: buying long premium into elevated IV before earnings, then losing on the post-event volatility crush even when the direction was right. Always check IV rank before choosing buy-vs-sell. Being right on direction but wrong on volatility is one of the most common ways an options thesis still loses money.
▸Try It

Goal: drive the grid on live-style scenarios.

  1. Bearish on NOVA with IV in the 90th percentile: pick a strategy from the grid and write its legs and break-even.
  2. Neutral on ACME with IV in the 15th percentile before a catalyst: choose between a calendar and a butterfly and justify it.
  3. For each, state explicitly how the trade benefits from the volatility regime, not just the direction.
